title: CSV-to-JSON Record Mapper
description: Given the sample CSV row 6,Learning Graph,5|4,FOUND, the learner can trace, one click at a time, how each field maps into the generated JSON node record and the two generated edge records, and correctly predict the JSON output for a new, unseen CSV row.
creator: Learning Graphs
author: Dan McCreary
subject: Learning Graphs

educational.topic: CSV to JSON Conversion, Node Records, Edge Records, Data Transformation
learningObjectives: Name which JSON key each CSV field becomes
Predict the complete JSON output for an unseen CSV row, including the correct edge count
Explain why one row produces exactly one node record but zero or more edge records
Explain what the pipe character carries that CSV itself cannot

pedagogical.teachingStrategy: Step-through worked example with a try-your-own-input check
keyQuestions: Three fields map to one JSON key each. Why does Dependencies map to something else entirely?
How many edge records does 1,Concept,,FOUND produce, and why?
Why can one row never produce two node records?
Where does the Group Record come from, if not from this row?
commonMisconceptions: That each CSV row maps to exactly one JSON record — a row with two dependencies yields three records
That an empty Dependencies cell produces an edge with an empty 'to' value — it produces no edge at all
That the pipe is part of the ConceptID value rather than a delimiter inside the cell
That the group record is generated per row — it is generated once per distinct TaxonomyID across the whole file
assessmentOpportunities: Ask learners to name the target JSON key before each Next Step click
Give an unseen row on paper and have learners write the full JSON before checking with Transform
Ask what 1,Concept,,FOUND produces and why the edge cardinality is 'zero or more'
